Questions.Title: Calculating the mean of a dataset
Questions.QuestionText: Given the dataset [4, 8, 6, 5, 3], what is the mean?
Questions.Hint: Sum all values and divide by the number of observations.
Questions.Feedback: The mean is calculated as (4+8+6+5+3)/5 = 26/5 = 5.2.

Options.OptionFeedback: Correct! The sum is 26 and dividing by 5 gives 5.2.
Incorrect. Make sure to sum all values correctly before dividing.
Incorrect. Re-check your addition of all five values.
Incorrect. Remember to divide the total sum by the number of observations.

Questions.Title: Median of an odd-numbered dataset
Questions.QuestionText: What is the median of the dataset [3, 4, 5, 6, 8]?
Questions.Hint: Arrange in order and find the middle value.
Questions.Feedback: The dataset is already sorted. With 5 values, the middle (3rd) value is 5, making the median 5.

Options.OptionFeedback: Correct! The middle value of this five-element sorted dataset is 5.
Incorrect. That is the mean, not the median.
Incorrect. The median is the single middle value for an odd-count dataset.
Incorrect. Count the positions carefully — the 3rd of 5 values is 5, not 6.

Questions.Title: Effect of outliers on the mean
Questions.QuestionText: Which statement best describes the sensitivity of the mean to extreme values (outliers)?
Questions.Hint: Think about what happens to the average when one very large value is added.
Questions.Feedback: The mean is sensitive to outliers because it includes every value in its calculation, so extreme values pull it toward the tail.

Options.Text: The mean is sensitive to outliers and can be skewed by extreme values.
Options.OptionFeedback: Correct! Outliers inflate or deflate the mean, making it unrepresentative.

Options.Text: The mean is unaffected by outliers because it uses the middle value.
Options.OptionFeedback: Incorrect. That describes the median, not the mean.

Options.Text: The mean always equals the median regardless of outliers.
Options.OptionFeedback: Incorrect. Outliers create a gap between the mean and median.

Options.Text: The mean is less sensitive to outliers than the median.
Options.OptionFeedback: Incorrect. The opposite is true — the median is more robust to outliers.

Questions.Title: Interpreting mean vs. median relationship
Questions.QuestionText: If the mean of a dataset is greater than the median, what does this indicate about the distribution?
Questions.Hint: Consider which direction the tail extends when the mean is pulled higher.
Questions.Feedback: When the mean exceeds the median, extreme high values pull the mean upward, indicating a right-skewed (positively skewed) distribution.

Options.Text: The distribution is likely right-skewed (positive skew).
Options.OptionFeedback: Correct! A mean greater than the median signals a tail extending toward higher values.

Options.Text: The distribution is likely left-skewed (negative skew).
Options.OptionFeedback: Incorrect. Left skew occurs when the mean is less than the median.

Options.Text: The distribution is perfectly symmetric.
Options.OptionFeedback: Incorrect. A symmetric distribution has a mean roughly equal to the median.

Options.Text: The distribution has no outliers.
Options.OptionFeedback: Incorrect. The relationship between mean and median reflects skewness, not the absence of outliers.

Questions.Title: Python function for computing median
Questions.QuestionText: Which NumPy function is used to compute the median of an array in Python?
Questions.Hint: NumPy has dedicated functions named after the statistics they compute.
Questions.Feedback: numpy.median() computes the median of an array, while numpy.mean() computes the mean.

Options.Text: numpy.median()
Options.OptionFeedback: Correct! numpy.median() returns the median value of the array.

Options.Text: numpy.mean()
Options.OptionFeedback: Incorrect. numpy.mean() computes the arithmetic average, not the median.

Options.Text: numpy.std()
Options.OptionFeedback: Incorrect. numpy.std() computes the standard deviation.

Options.Text: numpy.var()
Options.OptionFeedback: Incorrect. numpy.var() computes the variance.

Questions.Title: Why median is preferred for income data
Questions.QuestionText: Why is the median household income typically reported instead of the mean household income?
Questions.Hint: Think about how a small number of very high earners affect the average.
Questions.Feedback: A small number of extremely high earners inflate the mean, making it unrepresentative of a typical household. The median is more robust to these extreme values.

Options.Text: Because a small number of very high earners inflate the mean, making it unrepresentative.
Options.OptionFeedback: Correct! The median is not pulled by extreme values and better reflects the typical household.

Options.Text: Because the mean cannot be calculated for income data.
Options.OptionFeedback: Incorrect. The mean can always be calculated; it just may be misleading here.

Options.Text: Because the median is always larger than the mean for income data.
Options.OptionFeedback: Incorrect. For right-skewed income data, the mean is typically larger than the median.

Options.Text: Because income data never contains outliers.
Options.OptionFeedback: Incorrect. Income data frequently contains extreme outliers on the high end.

Questions.Title: Definition of variance
Questions.QuestionText: What does variance measure in a dataset?
Questions.Hint: Think about how each data point compares to the mean, squared.
Questions.Feedback: Variance measures the average squared difference between each data point and the mean, summarizing overall dispersion in the dataset.

Options.Text: The average squared difference between each data point and the mean.
Options.OptionFeedback: Correct! Variance squares the deviations from the mean and averages them.

Options.Text: The difference between the maximum and minimum values.
Options.OptionFeedback: Incorrect. That describes the range, not the variance.

Options.Text: The square root of the average deviation from the mean.
Options.OptionFeedback: Incorrect. That describes the standard deviation, not the variance.

Options.Text: The middle value of the squared dataset.
Options.OptionFeedback: Incorrect. Variance is not related to a middle value.

Questions.Title: Bessel's correction in sample variance
Questions.QuestionText: Why does sample variance use N-1 in the denominator instead of N?
Questions.Hint: Consider what happens when you estimate a population parameter from a sample.
Questions.Feedback: Using N-1 (Bessel's correction) produces an unbiased estimate of the population variance from a sample, correcting for the tendency to underestimate variability.

Options.Text: To produce an unbiased estimate of the population variance.
Options.OptionFeedback: Correct! N-1 corrects the systematic underestimation of variability in sample data.

Options.Text: To make the calculation easier by reducing the denominator.
Options.OptionFeedback: Incorrect. The purpose is statistical unbiasedness, not computational simplicity.

Options.Text: Because sample datasets always have one missing observation.
Options.OptionFeedback: Incorrect. N-1 is a mathematical correction, not an adjustment for missing data.

Options.Text: To inflate the variance so it is always larger than the standard deviation.
Options.OptionFeedback: Incorrect. That is not the purpose of Bessel's correction.

Questions.Title: Standard deviation vs. variance units
Questions.QuestionText: Why is standard deviation often preferred over variance as a measure of spread?
Questions.Hint: Think about the units of measurement after squaring versus taking a square root.
Questions.Feedback: Standard deviation is the square root of variance, which returns the measure of spread to the original units of the data, making it more intuitive to interpret.

Options.Text: It returns the measure of spread to the original units of the data.
Options.OptionFeedback: Correct! Taking the square root undoes the squaring, giving interpretable units.

Options.Text: It is always a smaller number than the variance.
Options.OptionFeedback: Incorrect. Standard deviation is smaller only when variance exceeds 1; this is not the primary reason.

Options.Text: It is easier to compute than variance.
Options.OptionFeedback: Incorrect. Standard deviation requires an extra square root step, making it slightly harder to compute.

Options.Text: It is not affected by outliers in the dataset.
Options.OptionFeedback: Incorrect. Standard deviation, like variance, is sensitive to outliers.

Questions.Title: NumPy parameter for sample standard deviation
Questions.QuestionText: When using numpy.std() to compute the sample standard deviation, which parameter must be set?
Questions.Hint: Think about Bessel's correction and how NumPy implements it.
Questions.Feedback: Setting ddof=1 in numpy.std() applies Bessel's correction, producing the sample standard deviation rather than the population standard deviation.

Options.Text: ddof=1
Options.OptionFeedback: Correct! ddof=1 tells NumPy to use N-1 in the denominator for an unbiased sample estimate.

Options.Text: ddof=0
Options.OptionFeedback: Incorrect. ddof=0 (the default) computes population standard deviation, not sample.

Options.Text: axis=1
Options.OptionFeedback: Incorrect. The axis parameter controls the dimension along which the calculation is performed, not the bias correction.

Options.Text: sample=True
Options.OptionFeedback: Incorrect. There is no 'sample=True' parameter in numpy.std(); use ddof=1.

Questions.Title: Skewness of a symmetric distribution
Questions.QuestionText: What is the skewness value of a perfectly symmetric distribution, such as the normal distribution?
Questions.Hint: Consider what asymmetry value means there is no asymmetry at all.
Questions.Feedback: A perfectly symmetric distribution has equal tails on both sides of the mean, resulting in a skewness of zero.

Options.Text: Zero
Options.OptionFeedback: Correct! Symmetry means no directional asymmetry, so skewness equals zero.

Options.Text: One
Options.OptionFeedback: Incorrect. A skewness of one indicates moderate positive asymmetry.

Options.Text: Negative one
Options.OptionFeedback: Incorrect. A skewness of negative one indicates moderate negative asymmetry.

Options.Text: It depends on the mean.
Options.OptionFeedback: Incorrect. Skewness measures shape, not the location of the mean.

Questions.Title: Real-world example of positive skewness
Questions.QuestionText: Which of the following is a classic real-world example of a positively skewed distribution?
Questions.Hint: Think about a distribution where most values cluster low but a few are extremely high.
Questions.Feedback: Income distributions are positively skewed because most people earn moderate incomes while a small number earn very high incomes, creating a long right tail.

Options.Text: Income distribution, where most earn moderate incomes but a few earn very high incomes.
Options.OptionFeedback: Correct! The long right tail of high earners creates positive skewness.

Options.Text: Exam scores where most students score very high but a few score very low.
Options.OptionFeedback: Incorrect. That scenario describes negative (left) skewness.

Options.Text: Heights of adult men, which follow a roughly normal distribution.
Options.OptionFeedback: Incorrect. Heights are approximately symmetric with near-zero skewness.

Options.Text: Test scores that cluster tightly around the mean.
Options.OptionFeedback: Incorrect. Tight clustering around the mean suggests low skewness and low spread.

Questions.Title: Python function for computing skewness
Questions.QuestionText: Which Python function from the SciPy library is used to compute sample skewness?
Questions.Hint: SciPy's statistics module contains a function named after the statistic it computes.
Questions.Feedback: scipy.stats.skew() computes sample skewness using Fisher's definition by default, which is the standard approach for data analysis.

Options.Text: scipy.stats.skew()
Options.OptionFeedback: Correct! scipy.stats.skew() returns the sample skewness of the data.

Options.Text: scipy.stats.kurtosis()
Options.OptionFeedback: Incorrect. kurtosis() measures the tailedness of the distribution, not skewness.

Options.Text: scipy.stats.norm()
Options.OptionFeedback: Incorrect. scipy.stats.norm is used for normal distribution calculations, not skewness.

Options.Text: scipy.stats.variance()
Options.OptionFeedback: Incorrect. There is no scipy.stats.variance(); and variance is not skewness.

Questions.Title: Discrete vs. continuous distributions
Questions.QuestionText: Which of the following is an example of a discrete probability distribution?
Questions.Hint: Think about whether the random variable can only take countable values.
Questions.Feedback: The Binomial distribution models the number of successes in a fixed number of trials, which are countable (discrete) outcomes. Human height is continuous.

Options.Text: The Binomial distribution modeling the number of successes in trials.
Options.OptionFeedback: Correct! Counts of successes are discrete, whole-number values.

Options.Text: The distribution of human heights.
Options.OptionFeedback: Incorrect. Height is a continuous variable and follows a continuous distribution.

Options.Text: The distribution of temperature readings.
Options.OptionFeedback: Incorrect. Temperature is a continuous variable.

Options.Text: The distribution of blood pressure measurements.
Options.OptionFeedback: Incorrect. Blood pressure is modeled as a continuous variable.

Questions.Title: Parameters defining the normal distribution
Questions.QuestionText: Which two parameters fully define a normal distribution?
Questions.Hint: One controls where the curve is centered, and the other controls how wide it is.
Questions.Feedback: The normal distribution is fully defined by the mean (μ), which controls location, and the standard deviation (σ), which controls the spread of the curve.

Options.Text: Mean (μ) and standard deviation (σ)
Options.OptionFeedback: Correct! These two parameters determine the location and spread of the normal curve.

Options.Text: Variance and skewness
Options.OptionFeedback: Incorrect. While related, the standard parameters of a normal distribution are mean and standard deviation.

Options.Text: Median and range
Options.OptionFeedback: Incorrect. The normal distribution is parameterized by mean and standard deviation, not median and range.

Options.Text: Number of trials (n) and probability of success (p)
Options.OptionFeedback: Incorrect. Those are the parameters of the binomial distribution, not the normal distribution.

Questions.Title: Empirical rule and standard deviations
Questions.QuestionText: According to the empirical rule, approximately what percentage of data falls within two standard deviations of the mean in a normal distribution?
Questions.Hint: The empirical rule is also called the 68-95-99.7 rule — each number corresponds to one, two, and three standard deviations.
Questions.Feedback: The empirical rule states that approximately 95% of data falls within two standard deviations of the mean in a normal distribution.

Options.OptionFeedback: Correct! Two standard deviations capture about 95% of the data.
Incorrect. 68% corresponds to one standard deviation from the mean.
Incorrect. 99.7% corresponds to three standard deviations from the mean.
Incorrect. 90% is not part of the standard empirical rule percentages.

Questions.Title: Key conditions for binomial distribution
Questions.QuestionText: Which situation would VIOLATE the conditions required to apply the binomial distribution?
Questions.Hint: Think about what happens when trials are not truly independent.
Questions.Feedback: Sampling without replacement from a small population makes trials dependent on each other, violating the independence condition. The hypergeometric distribution would be more appropriate in that case.

Options.Text: Sampling without replacement from a small population.
Options.OptionFeedback: Correct! This creates dependent trials, violating a key binomial condition.

Options.Text: Flipping a fair coin 10 times and counting heads.
Options.OptionFeedback: Incorrect. Coin flips are independent with a constant probability — a valid binomial experiment.

Options.Text: Testing 50 products where each has a known constant defect probability.
Options.OptionFeedback: Incorrect. This satisfies all binomial conditions.

Options.Text: Counting the number of correct answers on a 20-question true/false test with random guessing.
Options.OptionFeedback: Incorrect. Each question is independent with equal probability, satisfying binomial conditions.

Questions.Title: Mean and variance of binomial distribution
Questions.QuestionText: For a binomial distribution with n = 100 trials and p = 0.3, what is the expected number of successes (mean)?
Questions.Hint: The mean of a binomial distribution is calculated using a simple formula involving n and p.
Questions.Feedback: The mean of a binomial distribution is n × p = 100 × 0.3 = 30.

Options.OptionFeedback: Correct! Mean = n × p = 100 × 0.3 = 30.
Incorrect. 0.3 is the probability of success per trial, not the expected total.
Incorrect. That would be n × (1-p), representing the expected failures.
Incorrect. Re-apply the formula: mean = n × p.

Questions.Title: Poisson distribution mean and variance
Questions.QuestionText: What is a distinctive property of the Poisson distribution regarding its mean and variance?
Questions.Hint: Think about a mathematical relationship that is unique to the Poisson distribution.
Questions.Feedback: In the Poisson distribution, the mean and variance are both equal to λ (lambda). This equality is a useful diagnostic: if sample mean ≈ sample variance, a Poisson model may be appropriate.

Options.Text: The mean and variance are both equal to λ.
Options.OptionFeedback: Correct! This equality of mean and variance is a defining property of the Poisson distribution.

Options.Text: The mean is always greater than the variance.
Options.OptionFeedback: Incorrect. In the Poisson distribution, mean equals variance.

Options.Text: The variance is the square root of the mean.
Options.OptionFeedback: Incorrect. The standard deviation is the square root of the variance (λ), but variance equals the mean.

Options.Text: The mean and variance are both equal to n × p.
Options.OptionFeedback: Incorrect. n × p describes the mean of a binomial distribution, not a Poisson distribution.

Questions.Title: Computing Poisson probabilities in Python
Questions.QuestionText: Which SciPy function computes the cumulative probability P(X ≤ k) for a Poisson distribution?
Questions.Hint: Think about the difference between a probability mass function and a cumulative distribution function.
Questions.Feedback: poisson.cdf(k, mu=λ) computes the cumulative probability P(X ≤ k), while poisson.pmf(k, mu=λ) computes the probability of exactly k events.

Options.Text: scipy.stats.poisson.cdf(k, mu=λ)
Options.OptionFeedback: Correct! The CDF gives the cumulative probability P(X ≤ k).

Options.Text: scipy.stats.poisson.pmf(k, mu=λ)
Options.OptionFeedback: Incorrect. pmf gives the probability of exactly k events, not the cumulative probability.

Options.Text: scipy.stats.norm.cdf(k, mu=λ)
Options.OptionFeedback: Incorrect. norm.cdf is for the normal distribution, not the Poisson distribution.

Options.Text: scipy.stats.binom.cdf(k, n, p)
Options.OptionFeedback: Incorrect. binom.cdf is for the binomial distribution, not the Poisson distribution.
